title: Error probabilities and power
description: Practice reasoning about the probabilities of Type I and Type II errors and power in significance testing.

assessment_items.question_content:
A significance test is going to be performed using a significance level of $\alpha=0.05$. Suppose that the null hypothesis is actually false.

**If the significance level was lowered to $\alpha=0.01$, which of the following would be true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Since we are assuming that the null hypothesis is false, the correct conclusion would be to reject the null hypothesis.

Rejecting a false null hypothesis is more likely to happen with a higher significance level and less likely to happen with a lower significance level, since rejecting with lower significance requires our sample result to be farther away from the null hypothesis.

![Two overlapping normal distribution curves are plotted on the same x axis. The left curve has a mean of H sub 0. The right curve has mean, mu. A sample result is marked to the right of mu on the right curve. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing the rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4dc00fcbad4856951e49108241e5a7dee1af659b)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/028ab0ba297ce1a2d4d3c7d697f86e7917ef2f90)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked even further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/36d552a7a50eee4db35c2043fb62b5b45afed711)

A Type II error would occur if we failed to reject the false null hypothesis. Lowering the significance level makes it harder to reject a false null hypothesis, so a lower significance level would increase the probability of a Type II error.

Power is the likelihood that the test rejects the false null hypothesis. Lowering the significance level makes it harder to reject a false null hypothesis, so a lower significance level would decrease the power of the test.

###Answer

If we use a lower significance level, the probability of a Type II error would increase and the power of the test would decrease.

choices.content: Both the probability of a Type II error and the power of the test would decrease.
Both the probability of a Type II error and the power of the test would increase.
The probability of a Type II error would increase and the power of the test would decrease.
The probability of a Type II error would decrease and the power of the test would increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
A significance test is going to be performed using a significance level of $\alpha=0.05$. Suppose that the null hypothesis is actually false.

**If the significance level was raised to $\alpha=0.10$, which of the following would be true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Since we are assuming that the null hypothesis is false, the correct conclusion would be to reject the null hypothesis.

Rejecting a false null hypothesis is more likely to happen with a higher significance level and less likely to happen with a lower significance level, since rejecting with lower significance requires our sample result to be farther away from the null hypothesis.

![Two overlapping normal distribution curves are plotted on the same x axis. The left curve has a mean of H sub 0. The right curve has mean, mu. A sample result is marked to the right of mu on the right curve. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing the rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4dc00fcbad4856951e49108241e5a7dee1af659b)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/028ab0ba297ce1a2d4d3c7d697f86e7917ef2f90)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked even further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/36d552a7a50eee4db35c2043fb62b5b45afed711)

A Type II error would occur if we failed to reject the false null hypothesis. Lowering the significance level makes it harder to reject a false null hypothesis, so a lower significance level would increase the probability of a Type II error.

Power is the likelihood that the test rejects the false null hypothesis. Lowering the significance level makes it harder to reject a false null hypothesis, so a lower significance level would decrease the power of the test.

###Answer

If we raise the significance level, the probability of a Type II error would decrease and the power of the test would increase.

choices.content: Both the probability of a Type II error and the power of the test would decrease.
Both the probability of a Type II error and the power of the test would increase.
The probability of a Type II error would increase and the power of the test would decrease.
The probability of a Type II error would decrease and the power of the test would increase.

assessment_items.question_content:
A significance test is going to be performed using a significance level of $\alpha=0.05$. Suppose that the null hypothesis is actually true.

**If the significance level was lowered to $\alpha=0.01$, which of the following would be true?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a Type I error?

A Type I error is when we reject a true null hypothesis.

###Impact of significance level

Lowering the significance level $\alpha$ reduces the likelihood of a Type I error, because a lower alpha makes it harder to reject a true null hypothesis by random chance alone.

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of H sub 0. A sample result is marked to the right of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing the rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/11a6305f679826682f5e2906c2b2cb2ff66b8329)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. A sample result is marked further to the right of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/896639e1d0d37733d565b64a15e549c3ea64d951)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. A sample result is marked even further to the right of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b6fc7901ab4e38eccb7c0e0522ca4c76781738dd)

###Answer

If we use a lower significance level, the probability of a Type I error would decrease.

choices.content: The probability of a Type I error would increase.
The probability of a Type I error would decrease.
The probability of a Type I error would stay the same.
We don't have enough information to reason about Type I error probabilities.

assessment_items.question_content:
Asha owns a car wash and is trying to decide whether or not to purchase a vending machine so customers can buy coffee while they wait. She'll get the machine if she's convinced that more than $30\%$ of her customers would buy coffee. She plans on taking a random sample of $n$ customers and asking them whether or not they would buy coffee from the machine, and she'll then do a significance test using $\alpha=0.05$ to see if the sample proportion who say "yes" is significantly greater than $30\%$.

**Which situation below would result in the highest power for her test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is power?

Power is the likelihood that our sample result leads us to correctly reject a false null hypothesis.

###Impact of sample size

Larger sample sizes increase power.

If the null hypothesis is false, that means the proportion of customers who would buy coffee is something higher than $30\%$. Since results from larger samples vary less, a larger sample size makes it more likely for the sample result to be closer to the true value (something larger than $30\%$) than to the value in the null hypothesis — $30\%$.

###Impact of actual value

Power is higher when the actual value is farther away from the value in the null hypothesis and in favor of the alternative.

* If $30\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee, then the null isn't false, and Asha shouldn't reject the null.
* If $35\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee, then the null is false, but the sample result could plausibly come out to be close to $30\%$, which would lead her to fail to reject the null and make a Type II error.
* If $50\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee, then the null is false, and it would be unlikely for the sample result to be close to $30\%$. The sample result will probably come out closer to $50\%$, and that would lead her to reject $30\%.$

###Answer

Of these choices, the one that would result in the highest power for her test is a sample size of $n=200$, and $50\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee.

choices.content: She uses a sample size of $n=100$, and $32\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee.
She uses a sample size of $n=200$, and $32\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee.
She uses a sample size of $n=50$, and $50\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee.
She uses a sample size of $n=200$, and $50\%$ of all customers would actually buy coffee.

assessment_items.question_content:
Asha owns a car-wash and is trying to decide whether or not to purchase a vending machine so customers can buy coffee while they wait. She'll get the machine if she's convinced that more than $30\%$ of her customers would buy coffee. She plans on taking a random sample of $n$ customers and asking them whether or not they would buy coffee from the machine, and she'll then do a significance test using $\alpha=0.05$ to see if the sample proportion who say "yes" is significantly greater than $30\%$.

Suppose that in reality, it is actually $33\%$ of her customers that would buy coffee.

**Which of the changes below would result in the highest power for her test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is power?

Power is the likelihood that our sample result leads us to correctly reject a false null hypothesis.

###Impact of significance level

Rejecting a false null hypothesis is more likely to happen with a higher significance level and less likely to happen with a lower significance level, since rejecting with lower significance requires our sample result to be farther away from the null hypothesis.

![Two overlapping normal distribution curves are plotted on the same x axis. The left curve has a mean of H sub 0. The right curve has mean, mu. A sample result is marked to the right of mu on the right curve. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing the rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4dc00fcbad4856951e49108241e5a7dee1af659b)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/028ab0ba297ce1a2d4d3c7d697f86e7917ef2f90)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked even further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/36d552a7a50eee4db35c2043fb62b5b45afed711)

###Impact of sample size

Larger sample sizes increase power.

If the null hypothesis is false, that means the proportion of customers who would buy coffee is something higher than $30\%$. Since results from larger samples vary less, a larger sample size makes it more likely for the sample result to be closer to the true value (something larger than $30\%$) than to the value in the null hypothesis — $30\%$.

###Answer

Of these changes, the one that would result in the highest power for her test is increasing the significance level to $\alpha=0.10$ and using a sample size of $n=300$.

choices.content: Increase the significance level to $\alpha=0.10$ and use a sample size of $n=150$.
Increase the significance level to $\alpha=0.10$ and use a sample size of $n=300$.
Decrease the significance level to $\alpha=0.01$ and use a sample size of $n=150$.
Decrease the significance level to $\alpha=0.01$ and use a sample size of $n=300$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ricky is testing soil for a contaminant at a building site. He'll take action to stop construction if there's strong evidence that the soil has more than $400$ parts per million (ppm) of the contaminant. He plans on using soil from $n=30$ randomly selected locations at the building site. His hypotheses are $H_0: \mu \leq 400 \text{ ppm}$ and $H_{\text{a}}: \mu > 400 \text{ ppm}$, where $\mu$ is the mean amount of the contaminant in the soil at this site.

Suppose that in reality, $H_\text{a}$ is actually true.

**Which situation below would result in the lowest probability of a Type II error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a Type II error?

Ricky would make a Type II error if he failed to reject a false null hypothesis.

###Impact of significance level

Rejecting a false null hypothesis is more likely to happen with a higher significance level and less likely to happen with a lower significance level, since rejecting with lower significance requires our sample result to be farther away from the null hypothesis.

![Two overlapping normal distribution curves are plotted on the same x axis. The left curve has a mean of H sub 0. The right curve has mean, mu. A sample result is marked to the right of mu on the right curve. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing the rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4dc00fcbad4856951e49108241e5a7dee1af659b)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/028ab0ba297ce1a2d4d3c7d697f86e7917ef2f90)

![The previous overlapping distribution curves are displayed. On the right curve, a sample result is marked even further to the right of mu. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/36d552a7a50eee4db35c2043fb62b5b45afed711)

###Impact of actual value

The probability of a Type II error is lower when the actual value is farther away from the value in the null hypothesis and in favor of the alternative.

* If the mean is actually $400\text{ ppm}$, then the null isn't false, and Ricky shouldn't reject the null.
* If the mean is actually $405\text{ ppm}$, then the null is false, but the sample mean could plausibly come out to be close to $400\text{ ppm}$, which would lead him to fail to reject the null and make a Type II error.
* If the mean is actually $420\text{ ppm}$, then the null is false, and it would be less likely for the sample result to be close to $400\text{ ppm}$. The sample mean will probably come out closer to $420\text{ ppm}$, and that would lead him to reject $400\text{ ppm}$.

###Answer

Of these choices, the one that would result the lowest probability of a Type II error is if the true mean is actually $420 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a significance level of $\alpha =0.10$.

choices.content: The true mean is actually $405 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a significance level of $\alpha =0.05$.
The true mean is actually $405 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a significance level of $\alpha =0.10$.
The true mean is actually $420 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a significance level of $\alpha =0.05$.
The true mean is actually $420 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a significance level of $\alpha =0.10$.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ricky is testing soil for a contaminant at a building site. He'll take action to stop construction if there's strong evidence that the soil has more than $400$ parts per million (ppm) of the contaminant. He plans on using soil from $n$ randomly selected locations at the building site. His hypotheses are $H_0: \mu \leq 400 \text{ ppm}$ and $H_{\text{a}}: \mu > 400 \text{ ppm}$, where $\mu$ is the mean amount of the contaminant in the soil at this site. He's decided to use a significance level of $\alpha=0.05.$

Suppose that in reality, $H_\text{a}$ is actually true.

**Which situation below would result in the lowest probability of a Type II error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a Type II error?

Ricky would make a Type II error if he failed to reject a false null hypothesis.

###Impact of sample size

Larger sample sizes decrease the probability of a Type II error.

If the null hypothesis is false, then the mean is something higher than $400\text{ ppm}$. Since results from larger samples vary less, a larger sample size makes it more likely for the sample mean to be closer to the true value (something larger than $400\text{ ppm}$) than to the value in the null hypothesis — $400\text{ ppm}$.

###Impact of actual value

The probability of a Type II error is lower when the actual value is farther away from the value in the null hypothesis and in favor of the alternative.

* If the mean is actually $400\text{ ppm}$, then the null isn't false, and Ricky shouldn't reject the null.
* If the mean is actually $405\text{ ppm}$, then the null is false, but the sample mean could plausibly come out to be close to $400\text{ ppm}$, which would lead him to fail to reject the null and make a Type II error.
* If the mean is actually $420\text{ ppm}$, then the null is false, and it would be less likely for the sample result to be close to $400\text{ ppm}$. The sample mean will probably come out closer to $420\text{ ppm}$, and that would lead him to reject $400\text{ ppm}$.

###Answer

Of these choices, the one that would result the lowest probability of a Type II error is if the true mean is actually $420 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a sample size of $n =50$.

choices.content: The true mean is actually $420 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a sample size of $n =30$.
The true mean is actually $420 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a sample size of $n =50$.
The true mean is actually $405 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a sample size of $n =30$.
The true mean is actually $405 \text{ ppm}$, and he uses a sample size of $n =50$.

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a report from the United States Environmental Protection Agency, burning one gallon of gasoline typically emits about $8.9\text{ kg}$ of $\text{CO}_2$. A fuel company wants to test a new type of gasoline designed to have lower $\text{CO}_2$ emissions. Their hypotheses are $H_0: \mu =8.9 \text{ kg}$ and $H_{\text{a}}: \mu < 8.9 \text{ kg}$, where $\mu$ is the mean amount of $\text{CO}_2$ emitted by burning one gallon of this new gasoline.

Suppose that $H_0$ is actually true.

**Which situation below would have the lowest probability of a Type I error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a Type I error?

The company would make a Type I error if they rejected a true null hypothesis.

###Impact of significance level

Lowering the significance level $\alpha$ reduces the likelihood of a Type I error, because a lower alpha makes it harder to reject a true null hypothesis by random chance alone.

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of H sub 0. A sample result is marked to the left of the mean. The area to the left of it is shaded, representing the rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/761939bf3dd7ca713d7d8d354d24313e9c5a844d)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. A sample result is marked further to the left of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/83a3c6eef7b46307b01e344c5a3907d124d76b30)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. A sample result is marked even further to the left of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4a3c58f084b326731781513197aeaf4e6d32b921)

###Impact of sample size

Sample size doesn't impact the likelihood of a Type I error. Larger samples are still preferred since they produce less variable results, but we'll still reject a true $H_0$ at a rate equal to the significance level $\alpha$.

###Answer

Of these choices, the one that would have the lowest probability of a Type I error is $n=20$ and $\alpha=0.01$.

choices.content: $n=20$ and $\alpha=0.01$
$n=50$ and $\alpha=0.05$
$n=75$ and $\alpha=0.10$

assessment_items.question_content:
A city planner will consider adding electric vehicle charging stations if she is convinced that more than $15\%$ of residents would use them. She will survey a sample of residents to test the hypotheses $H_0: p\leq 15\%$ vs. $H_\text{a}: p>15\%$, where $p$ is the true proportion of residents that would use the stations.

Suppose that $H_0$ is actually true.

**Which situation below would have the lowest probability of a Type I error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a Type I error?

The city planner would make a Type I error if she rejected a true null hypothesis.

###Impact of significance level

Lowering the significance level $\alpha$ reduces the likelihood of a Type I error, because a lower alpha makes it harder to reject a true null hypothesis by random chance alone.

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of H sub 0. A sample result is marked to the right of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing the rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/11a6305f679826682f5e2906c2b2cb2ff66b8329)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. A sample result is marked further to the right of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/896639e1d0d37733d565b64a15e549c3ea64d951)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. A sample result is marked even further to the right of the mean. The area to the right of it is shaded, representing alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b6fc7901ab4e38eccb7c0e0522ca4c76781738dd)

###Impact of sample size

Sample size doesn't impact the likelihood of a Type I error. Larger samples are still preferred since they produce less variable results, but we'll still reject a true $H_0$ at a rate equal to the significance level $\alpha$.

###Answer

Of these choices, the one that would have the lowest probability of a Type I error is $n=200$ and $\alpha=0.01$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A manufacturer makes ball bearings that have a target diameter of $5\text{ mm}$. Quality control engineers sample ball bearings from a batch to test the hypotheses $H_0: \mu = 5\text{ mm}$ vs. $H_\text{a}: \mu \neq 5\text{ mm}$, where $\mu$ is the true mean diameter of the ball bearings in that batch.

Suppose that $H_0$ is actually true.

**Which situation below would have the lowest probability of a Type I error?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a Type I error?

The engineers would make a Type I error if they rejected a true null hypothesis.

###Impact of significance level

Lowering the significance level $\alpha$ reduces the likelihood of a Type I error, because a lower alpha makes it harder to reject a true null hypothesis by random chance alone.

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of H sub 0. Two sample result are marked under the left and right tails, respectively. The areas beyond the values are shaded, with each representing a rejection region, alpha = 0.10.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8520d9ce02f7b577a77c453ce0d64ea4c414d64b)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. Two sample results are marked further from the mean beneath the left and right tails. The areas beyond the values are shaded, with each representing a rejection region, alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4d2884be22f03d4632fcdd0182ddbd007ebc0f54)

![The previous distribution curve is displayed. Two sample results are marked further from the mean beneath the left and right tails. The areas beyond the values are shaded, with each representing a rejection region, alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/100612919c84d77e60ffacac7ea0d3fc7de6cf52)

###Impact of sample size

Sample size doesn't impact the likelihood of a Type I error. Larger samples are still preferred since they produce less variable results, but we'll still reject a true $H_0$ at a rate equal to the significance level $\alpha$.

###Answer

Of these choices, the one that would have the lowest probability of a Type I error is $n=30$ and $\alpha=0.01$.

choices.content: $n=50$ and $\alpha=0.10$
$n=50$ and $\alpha=0.05$
$n=30$ and $\alpha=0.01$

assessment_items.question_content:

Suppose that $H_0$ is actually true.

[[☃ radio 1]]

###What is a Type I error?

The areas beyond the values are shaded, with each representing a rejection region, alpha = 0.05.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4d2884be22f03d4632fcdd0182ddbd007ebc0f54)

The areas beyond the values are shaded, with each representing a rejection region, alpha = 0.01.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/100612919c84d77e60ffacac7ea0d3fc7de6cf52)

###Impact of sample size

Sample size doesn't impact the likelihood of a Type I error. Larger samples are still preferred since they produce less variable results, but we'll still reject a true $H_0$ at a rate equal to the significance level $\alpha$.

Of these choices, the one that would have the lowest probability of a Type I error is $n=50$ and $\alpha=0.01$.
